title: PM Modi Concludes Three-Nation Tour in Oman with Strategic Bilateral Talks
lede: Prime Minister Narendra Modi concluded his three-nation tour in Oman on 18 December 2025, holding bilateral talks with Sultan Haitham bin Tarik following a formal reception that underscored deepening strategic engagement between India and the Sultanate.

sections.context: Prime Minister Modi arrived in Muscat on the afternoon of 17 December 2025 at the invitation of Sultan Haitham bin Tarik, marking the final leg of a three-nation diplomatic tour. The delegation was received at Muscat airport by the Deputy Prime Minister for Defence Affairs, His Highness Sayyid Shihab bin Tarik Al Said, accompanied by Cabinet Ministers and senior officials, with a ceremonial guard of honour accorded to the visiting leader. An official dinner banquet hosted by Oman's Deputy Prime Minister on the evening of 17 December preceded substantive bilateral engagement on 18 December, which included both a tête-à-tête between Modi and Sultan Haitham bin Tarik and formal delegation-level talks. This visit follows Sultan Haitham bin Tarik's visit to India in December 2023, establishing a pattern of high-level bilateral exchange and positioning the engagement as part of [[india-om-pm-modis-december-2025-oman-visit-strengthens-india-gul-2026|India's broader Gulf strategy recalibration]].
sections.threads: Modi's Oman visit integrates into India's wider Gulf engagement strategy, as evidenced by the three-nation tour structure and the formal protocol extended during this final diplomatic stop. The bilateral engagement reflects the continuation of diplomatic momentum established during the Sultan's India visit two years prior, suggesting sustained institutional prioritisation of the India-Oman relationship. The visit also signals India's engagement with key maritime and strategic partners in the Indian Ocean region, a connectivity concern that carries weight given the [[india-om-indian-crew-missing-after-attack-on-settebello-off-oman-2026|security incidents affecting Indian vessels in Omani waters]]. The timing and scale of the visit, as the concluding engagement of a three-nation tour, underscores Oman's diplomatic significance within India's regional architecture, distinguishing it from transactional bilateral arrangements.
sections.signal: Observers should monitor the substance of outcomes from the 18 December bilateral and delegation-level talks, which the official briefing does not detail in the available transcript excerpt. The pattern of reciprocal high-level visits—Sultan's 2023 India visit followed by Modi's 2025 Oman visit—suggests potential formalisation of engagement frameworks or sectoral partnerships in subsequent months. Critical attention should be directed toward whether this visit produces institutional mechanisms for deepening defence, maritime security, or energy cooperation, given the Deputy Prime Minister for Defence Affairs role in reception protocols and the formal nature of bilateral engagement.
sections.forward: The next signpost for India-Oman relations will be the announcement or formalisation of outcomes from Modi's December 2025 bilateral with Sultan Haitham bin Tarik. Analysts should track whether defence cooperation, maritime security arrangements, or energy partnerships feature in official statements or joint documents emerging from this visit. The visit's conclusion as Modi's three-nation tour finale suggests the bilateral outcome may carry broader regional significance, potentially signalling India's strategic posture in the Gulf and Arabian Sea region for the medium term.
